---
title: "Bahia Grass (Paspalum notatum)"
slug: "paspalum-notatum"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354728854/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Bahia Grass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354728854/medium.jpg" alt="Bahia Grass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/emmystew">
emmystew</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/200995919">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=166188)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in lawns, pastures, roadsides, and other disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A perennial grass characterized by its tough, spreading growth and distinctive Y-shaped seed heads. In Florida, it is commonly found in lawns, pastures, roadsides, and other disturbed areas, especially in sandy soils. It grows during the warmer months and is widely used for erosion control and forage, often forming dense, durable turf.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/166188-Paspalum-notatum)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/1301)
